channel.title: Bull & Bear With Me
channel.description: Markets, explained without the finance-bro energy. A daily 4-minute read for regular people.

item.title: Bull & Bear With Me — Apr 19, 2026
item.description:
*The Kelp DAO hack just ate $292 million and nobody at brunch mentioned it once.*
THE OPEN
Sunday morning. Markets are closed. Your 401(k) is sitting near all-time highs. And somewhere, a crypto hacker is having the best weekend of their life. We've got a lot of ground to cover.
BIG THREE
- The Strait of Hormuz story just keeps being the story. Iran opened it, oil cratered nearly 10%, stocks hit records, Bitcoin ticked up. We've been tracking this all week — last week Ken Griffin was saying a closed Strait meant global recession, and now oil is sitting under $95 and the market is throwing a ticker-tape parade. Brent crude is still at $95, which is elevated, but it's a long way from the $127 peak that was giving everyone ulcers. The war isn't over. The deal isn't signed. But the world's most important oil pipe is open again and markets are choosing optimism. Can't blame them.
- Netflix is still on the floor and hasn't gotten up. Down 9% after its earnings miss last week — not because revenue was bad, it wasn't, but because next quarter's forecast disappointed and Reed Hastings, the guy who literally started the whole thing, announced he's leaving the board in June. Wall Street treated a 16% revenue year like a participation trophy. The stock might bounce. Analysts say buy the dip. But right now Netflix is that running back who rushes for 110 yards and still gets benched because he fumbled at the goal line.
- The biggest DeFi hack of 2026 happened quietly this weekend and your group chat probably didn't mention it. Kelp DAO — a crypto platform — got drained of about $292 million overnight. DeFi stands for "decentralized finance," which basically means financial products running on code with no bank in the middle. Cool idea. Catastrophic when someone finds a hole in that code. The attacker walked out with 18% of the token's entire supply. Multiple platforms had to freeze operations. And Bitcoin and Ethereum both slipped on the news. This is the part of crypto that the "gonna make it" crowd glosses over. The technology is genuinely interesting. The security is genuinely not.
ONE THING WORTH UNDERSTANDING
The Fed is stuck and it's worth saying plainly one more time. The Fed controls interest rates — the lever that makes borrowing money more or less expensive for everyone. They want inflation at 2%. It's sitting at 3%. That gap means they can't lower rates without potentially making inflation worse. Now with oil still at $95 — well above pre-war levels — prices on stuff you actually buy aren't falling fast. The Fed's own people said this week they're in "wait and see" mode. Translation: your mortgage rate isn't dropping anytime soon. Your car loan either. The Strait being open helps. But it's not a cure.
THE CLOSE
Records on Wall Street. $292 million gone in DeFi. Oil falling. Rates frozen. It's Sunday. Touch some grass. We'll be back tomorrow.

item.title: Bull & Bear With Me — Apr 18, 2026
item.description:
*Iran opened the door, the Nasdaq went on a 13-game heater, and your 401(k) is having the best April of its life.*
THE OPEN
The market just finished one of the best weeks in recent memory and it's Saturday, so let's just sit with that for a second. S&P above 7,100. New record. Nasdaq on a 13-game win streak — longest since 1992, when most of our readers weren't paying bills yet. The reason for all of it is basically one body of water deciding to stop being a problem.
BIG THREE
- Iran declared the Strait of Hormuz open for business and the market absolutely lost its mind in the best possible way. All three major indexes hit all-time highs Friday. Bitcoin ripped. Oil cratered nearly 11% in a single day. Brent crude — the global oil price we talked about last week when it was threatening to end civilization — dipped below $90. Two weeks ago Ken Griffin was saying a closed Strait meant global recession. The Strait is open. The market threw a parade. That's where we are.
- The Nasdaq just did something it hasn't done since 1992: thirteen straight winning days. For context, in 1992, a gallon of gas cost $1.05 and the top movie was Home Alone. The streak is being driven by AI chip earnings, bank earnings, and general geopolitical relief. TSMC — the Taiwanese company that builds the actual chips inside every AI product you've ever heard of — posted a 58% jump in profit. Fifty-eight percent. The AI wave isn't vibes. It's showing up in the numbers.
- Netflix got punched in the mouth Thursday night and is still eating floor. Down 9% after hours after a disappointing forecast and news that Reed Hastings — the guy who literally founded the company — is leaving the board in June. Revenue was great. Wall Street didn't care. Analysts are saying buy the dip though, so if you've got Netflix in your brokerage account and you're staring at it with your stomach in your throat, you're not alone and the pros think it bounces back.
ONE THING WORTH UNDERSTANDING
Oil prices and your daily life are more connected than you think. When oil gets expensive, it costs more to move literally everything — trucks, ships, planes. That cost gets passed to you at the grocery store, the pump, the Amazon delivery. That's part of why inflation has been such a bastard. When oil drops — like it did Friday — that pressure eases. Slowly. Don't expect gas prices to fall next Tuesday. But if crude stays down near $90 instead of $127 where it peaked, inflation cools, the Fed feels less stressed, and rate cuts — which make mortgages and car loans cheaper — become more realistic. One strait. That's how connected this stuff all is.
THE CLOSE
Your 401(k) just had one of its best weeks in years. Oil is falling. The Nasdaq is on a historic run. Enjoy the Saturday. You earned it. We'll be back Monday.

item.title: Bull & Bear With Me — Apr 17, 2026
item.description:
*Ticketmaster is literally a criminal, your 401(k) is near all-time highs, and Netflix just had a rough Thursday night.*
THE OPEN
Markets finished the week green and quiet — like that one guy at the party who didn't drink but still had a good time. Nasdaq's on a 12-game winning streak. We take it.
BIG THREE
- The ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon is the real reason your 401(k) looks good right now. When Middle East tensions flare up, oil gets expensive, gas gets expensive, everything gets expensive, and stocks freak out. When things cool down, the whole machine exhales. We fell almost 10% in late March. We've clawed back more than that since. One diplomatic phone call is doing more for your retirement account than anything on CNBC.
- Netflix dropped 10% after hours and honestly it's a little embarrassing. Revenue up 16%, $12 billion in the door — that's a great quarter. But the guy who founded the company is leaving in June, and their forecast for next quarter underwhelmed some investors. Wall Street is the friend who gets you a great birthday gift and then gets mad you didn't thank them fast enough. The stock will probably be fine. But tonight it's eating dinner alone.
- TSMC — the Taiwanese company that makes the actual chips that power all your AI stuff — crushed earnings. Net income up 58% year over year. That matters because TSMC is basically the factory for every AI ambition on earth. If they're doing well, the AI wave is still real and not just vibes. Good sign.
ONE THING WORTH UNDERSTANDING
The retail investor pile-in is worth knowing about. "Retail investors" just means regular people — us. Not hedge funds, not Goldman Sachs. Just guys with a Robinhood account or a 401(k) they check on their phone. According to JPMorgan, regular people largely sat out this recent rally, watching from the sidelines. Now that the market's near record highs, they're jumping back in. That's historically when you want to be careful — not because you should sell everything, but because when everyone finally decides it's safe to pile in near the top, there's less room to run and more room to fall. Not a prediction. Just something to file away.
KEEP AN EYE ON
- Ticketmaster is now legally a monopoly, per a federal jury. Thirty-four states sued them. They lost. What happens next — whether they actually get broken up or just pay a fine and keep charging you $40 in fees to see a band you kind of like — that plays out over the next few months.
- Oil sitting near $92 a barrel means the Fed isn't cutting interest rates anytime soon. That means mortgage rates stay high. That means car loans stay expensive. The ceasefire is a start. Oil needs to come way down before any of that changes.
THE CLOSE
It's Friday. Your 401(k) had a good week. Netflix did not. Pour one for Reed Hastings. See you Monday.

item.title: Bull & Bear With Me — Apr 16, 2026
item.description:
*S&P 500 hit 7,000 for the first time ever. Meanwhile, a strait nobody knew existed might blow up the whole party.*
THE OPEN
Records on records — S&P crossed 7,000, Nasdaq's on an 11-day win streak — and somehow the whole thing is held together with a Middle East peace deal that may or may not be real.
BIG THREE
- The S&P 500 closed above 7,000 for the first time in history and the Nasdaq hasn't had a down day in nearly two weeks. Eleven straight days of gains. That's the market equivalent of going 11-0 in fantasy and then finding out your opponent next week is healthy again. Tech led everything — Tesla ripped 7% on AI chip hype, Meta and Broadcom announced a massive chip deal, banks posted strong earnings. Genuinely good news. Enjoy it.
- The party has one very large bouncer standing outside: the Strait of Hormuz. It's a narrow waterway between Iran and Oman — about as wide as a county road relative to how much oil flows through it — and right now it's effectively shut down. Twenty percent of the world's oil and gas normally moves through there. It's been closed for over a month. Brent crude, which is basically the global price tag for a barrel of oil, already hit $127 in early April. Ken Griffin — guy who runs one of the biggest hedge funds on earth — said flat out: if that strait stays closed six to twelve months, global recession is unavoidable. That's not a Reddit comment. That's one of the most plugged-in people in finance saying the quiet part loud.
- Netflix and PepsiCo reported today. Pepsi is a signal on whether regular people are still spending — and the numbers were fine but soft. Netflix matters because it tells you whether consumers are cutting the fun stuff yet. No dramatic swings either way. Consider it a yellow light, not a red one.
ONE THING WORTH UNDERSTANDING
The Fed is stuck, and here's why that matters to your actual life. The Fed controls interest rates — think of it as the dial that makes borrowing money cheaper or more expensive. When inflation is high, they turn the dial up to slow things down. When the economy is struggling, they turn it down to get things moving. Right now, inflation is sitting at 3% and the Fed wants it at 2%. That one-point gap sounds small but it means they can't lower rates without making inflation worse. So your mortgage, your car loan, your credit card — all still expensive. And with oil spiking because of the Strait situation, groceries and gas aren't coming down either. The Fed can't save you right now. They're just watching.
KEEP AN EYE ON
- Netflix earnings — dropped after close today. Subscriber numbers will tell you a lot about whether people are still in spending mode or quietly tightening up. - Any update on Iran ceasefire talks. Trump said it's "close to over." Markets believe him right now. If that changes, everything changes fast.
THE CLOSE
Records on Wall Street, a closed oil chokepoint, and a peace deal hanging by a thread. Normal Thursday stuff. Don't let the green numbers make you forget the Strait exists.

item.title: Bull & Bear With Me — Apr 15, 2026
item.description:
*Banks are printing money, quantum stocks went insane, and there might be actual peace in Iran — but inflation's still being a dick.*
THE OPEN
Markets had a genuinely good day yesterday and Wall Street is walking around like a guy who just hit a parlay. S&P hit a new all-time high. Nasdaq had its best 11-day run on record. Even Bitcoin remembered it exists. The vibe is cautiously optimistic, which in market-speak means everyone's one bad headline away from panicking again.
BIG THREE
- JPMorgan made $16.5 billion in a single quarter. Morgan Stanley beat estimates by a mile. Bank earnings are reminding us that whatever chaos is happening in the world, the big banks are doing just fine, thank you. They're basically the house at the casino. The rest of us are the players.
- Quantum computing stocks went absolutely feral. IonQ up 21%. D-Wave up 22%. Rigetti up 13%. One day. For context, quantum computing is basically regular computing's weird genius cousin who's been in the garage for thirty years and might finally be about to do something incredible — or might just be loud about it. A government contract and a technical breakthrough sent the whole sector into orbit. If you don't own any and you're staring at this number with your mouth open, welcome to the club.
- The U.S.-Iran situation is calming down — and that's moving everything. Oil dropped from $104 a barrel at the peak of the conflict down to $91. Pakistan's army chief flew to Tehran to help extend the ceasefire. Trump says it's almost over. Peace talks moving forward = oil prices dropping = gas prices maybe getting less insane = inflation pressure easing. This is the domino everyone's watching right now.
ONE THING WORTH UNDERSTANDING
Inflation is the reason you feel broke even when nothing obviously bad happened to you. When inflation is high, everything costs more — groceries, rent, gas — and your paycheck buys less of it. The Fed's job is to keep inflation around 2% by adjusting interest rates. When inflation runs hot, they raise rates to slow things down. Right now inflation is at 3.3% — higher than they want — and the Fed has rates parked at 3.50-3.75% trying to squeeze it back down. The problem is those high rates make mortgages expensive, car loans expensive, credit card debt more brutal. So it's not great either way. The hope was that they'd cut rates in 2026. Now that's looking shakier. That matters for your 401(k), your car payment, and anyone trying to buy a house.
KEEP AN EYE ON
- Netflix, PepsiCo, and Charles Schwab all report today. Netflix especially — if streaming is strong, it tells us whether regular people are still spending on stuff they don't need. Which, honestly, same. - Kevin Warsh's Fed Chair confirmation hearing hits the Senate on April 21. The next Fed chair will set interest rates for the next four years. Worth knowing his name.
THE CLOSE
Markets are up, banks are rich, quantum computers are having a moment, and there might be peace in the Middle East. Somehow inflation is still the buzzkill at the party. Classic inflation.

item.title: Bull & Bear With Me — Apr 14, 2026
item.description:
*The market almost forgot there's a war on.*
THE OPEN
Green across the board, earnings coming in strong, and your 401(k) is almost back to where it was before everything went sideways. Good morning. Don't get too comfortable.
BIG THREE
- The S&P 500 is now less than one percent from its all-time high. To put that in perspective: a few weeks ago we were watching it drop like a check engine light came on. Now it's basically recovered. The Nasdaq has gone up ten days in a row — its best streak in five years. Tech carried the whole thing, which means if you've got a 401(k) sitting in any kind of growth fund, you've been quietly having a good week whether you knew it or not.
- JPMorgan crushed earnings. Goldman Sachs had their second-best quarter ever. Then Goldman's stock went DOWN. That's Wall Street doing Wall Street things — "good news already priced in" is the finance version of "yeah, but." The bank put up historic numbers and traders shrugged like a guy who just got a raise but wanted a bigger one. Exhausting people.
- CarMax dropped 15% in one day. CarMax is the big used car dealership chain, and their numbers were rough compared to last year. Worth paying attention to if you're thinking about buying or selling a car soon, because what hurts CarMax usually means the used car market is softening. Could be good news for buyers. Just noting it.
ONE THING WORTH UNDERSTANDING
Inflation came in at 3.3% last month. Here's what that means in plain English: a thing that cost $100 a year ago now costs $103.30. The main culprit is gas prices — energy swings around a lot, which is why economists also look at "core inflation," which just strips out food and gas to get a cleaner read. Core came in at 2.6%. The Fed — the guys who set interest rates for the whole country — want inflation closer to 2% before they'll cut rates. Cutting rates is basically the Fed making it cheaper to borrow money, which tends to get the economy moving. At 3.3%, they're not doing that yet. There's a 97% chance they hold steady at their meeting on April 28th. So don't hold your breath for cheaper mortgages or car loans anytime soon.
KEEP AN EYE ON
- The Strait of Hormuz situation. That's the narrow waterway where a huge chunk of the world's oil moves through. The U.S. blockaded Iranian oil shipments there. One Wall Street CEO said if it stays shut for six to twelve months, the whole world goes into a recession. Iran apparently called Washington. Something's happening. We just don't know what yet.
- Jerome Powell is out as Fed chair on May 15th. His replacement has crypto in his personal portfolio. Make of that what you will.
THE CLOSE
Your retirement account recovered. Inflation is still annoying. There's a naval blockade happening. It's a lot for a Tuesday. Go touch grass.

item.title: Bull & Bear With Me — Apr 13, 2026
item.description:
**📈 The Open**
The U.S. just blockaded the Strait of Hormuz, oil hit $104 a barrel, inflation jumped to 3.3%, Goldman missed on bonds, and somehow the S&P is basically flat. Markets are fine. Everything is fine.
---
**🎯 Big Three**
- **The Strait of Hormuz is blockaded.** About 20% of the world's oil flows through that narrow waterway, and we just put a Navy cork in it. Gas was already $4.12 a gallon before this. Analysts are calling it the worst energy disruption since the 1970s, which is a hell of a thing to read before your Monday morning coffee.
- **Inflation jumped from 2.4% to 3.3% in one month.** That's not a drift. That's a lurch. The Fed has two jobs — keep inflation down and keep employment up — and right now both are quietly going sideways. Their response, per Vice Chair Jefferson: our policy is "well-positioned." Comforting stuff, truly.
- **Goldman Sachs had a near-record quarter and still dropped 4%.** Revenues up 14%. EPS beat. But the bond desk missed by $850 million and Wall Street punished them like they showed up to the company picnic without potato salad. The rest of the big banks — JPMorgan, Wells Fargo, Citi, BofA — report this week. Could be the most expensive week of earnings season to actually pay attention to.
---
**🧠 One Thing Worth Understanding**
**The Strait of Hormuz, and why it matters to your drive to work.**
The Strait of Hormuz is a narrow strip of water between Iran and Oman — about 21 miles wide at its tightest point. Something like 20-21% of all the world's oil passes through it every single day, tankers carrying crude from Saudi Arabia, Iraq, Kuwait, and the UAE to the rest of the planet. When that flow gets disrupted — whether by conflict, blockade, or threats — oil prices spike globally because traders don't wait around to see how it plays out. They panic-buy futures and the price jumps immediately, which is why WTI crude went up 8% in one morning. That feeds into gas prices within days, then into shipping costs, then into basically everything that gets transported or made with energy, which is everything. The 1970s oil crisis caused recessions across the Western world. We're not there yet. But the direction of travel is not great, Bob.
---
**👀 Keep An Eye On**
- **Bank earnings all week.** JPMorgan reports Tuesday and tends to set the tone. If they miss, expect the "soft landing" crowd to go quiet for a few days. - **Oil prices Monday night and Tuesday.** Any further escalation around the blockade could push WTI toward $110. That's the number that really starts showing up in everything.
---
**☕ The Close**
Markets are "pricing in the geopolitical risk," which is analyst-speak for "we have no idea what happens next, but we've agreed to be calm about it." Respect the process.
